coredata.dc:title: Effect of Pr concentration on the magnetic properties and phase evolution of Ti-substituted Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 (y =9.5–7) nanocomposite ribbons
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, H.W.
Chiu, C.H.
Chang, C.W.
Chang, W.C.
Yao, Y.D.
Sun, A.C.
coredata.dc:description: Magnetic properties, phase evolution, and microstructure of melt spun Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 nanocomposite ribbons with different Pr concentration (y =9.5–7) have been investigated. For Pr9.5Fe78.5Ti2B10, only the 2:14:1 phase and the α-Fe phase existed, while an additional Fe3B phase appears for the ribbons with y =9–7.5. On further decrease of the Pr content (y =7), one extra phase, 2:23:3, is found. The exchange coupling effect, as evidenced by a Henkel plot is found, to decrease with the decrease of the rare earth content, which is attributed to the increasing of grain size with decreasing of Pr content. The magnetic properties of the ribbons first improve with the decrease of y, reaching a maximum at y =8.5 (B r =9.6kG, i H c =8.5kOe, and (BH)max =17.8MGOe), then decrease with further decrease of the Pr content (B r =9.8kG, i H c =5.6kOe, and (BH)max =14.5MGOe for y =7). In comparison with the Ti-free Pr x Fe90−x B10 (x =9.5–7) ribbons, improved magnetic properties with higher intrinsic coercivity can be obtained in Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 nanocomposites. The maximum magnetic properties of Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 nanocomposites can be found in the ribbons with lower rare earth content, i.e. x =9.5 for the former and y =8.5 for the latter.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic properties
Phase evolution
Nanocomposite ribbons

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: B r, i H c, and (BH)max values as measured at 20°C of Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 ribbons (y =9.5–7) following their optimum crystallization treatment.
Thermal magnetic analysis of Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 ribbons.
Transmission electron microscopy revealing the microstructures of Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 ribbons.
The applied magnetic field dependence of δM (=m d(H)−(1−2m r(H))) of Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 ribbons.
Variation of i H c, and (BH)max as a function of Pr content for Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 and Pr x Fe90−x B10 ribbons.
B r, i H c, and (BH)max values as measured at 20°C of (Pr1−x Nd x )8.5Fe79.5Ti2B10 ribbons (x =0–0.5) following their optimum crystallization treatment

article.ce:title: Effect of Pr concentration on the magnetic properties and phase evolution of Ti-substituted Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 (y =9.5–7) nanocomposite ribbons

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Chung Cheng University, Chia-Yi, Taiwan, ROC

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physics, Academia Sinica, NanKang, Taipei 11529, Taiwan, ROC

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan, ROC

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnetic properties, phase evolution, and microstructure of melt spun Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 nanocomposite ribbons with different Pr concentration (y =9.5–7) have been investigated. For Pr9.5Fe78.5Ti2B10, only the 2:14:1 phase and the α-Fe phase existed, while an additional Fe3B phase appears for the ribbons with y =9–7.5. On further decrease of the Pr content (y =7), one extra phase, 2:23:3, is found. The exchange coupling effect, as evidenced by a Henkel plot is found, to decrease with the decrease of the rare earth content, which is attributed to the increasing of grain size with decreasing of Pr content. The magnetic properties of the ribbons first improve with the decrease of y, reaching a maximum at y =8.5 (B r =9.6kG, i H c =8.5kOe, and (BH)max =17.8MGOe), then decrease with further decrease of the Pr content (B r =9.8kG, i H c =5.6kOe, and (BH)max =14.5MGOe for y =7). In comparison with the Ti-free Pr x Fe90−x B10 (x =9.5–7) ribbons, improved magnetic properties with higher intrinsic coercivity can be obtained in Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 nanocomposites. The maximum magnetic properties of Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 nanocomposites can be found in the ribbons with lower rare earth content, i.e. x =9.5 for the former and y =8.5 for the latter.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanocomposites, such as α-Fe/R2Fe14B [1,2], and Fe3B/R2Fe14B [3,4], have attracted much attention for application in rare earth bonded magnets since the last decade, because of the improved magnetic properties, including remanence B r and maximum energy product (BH)max, induced by the exchange coupling effect between magnetically soft and hard phases. The boron concentrations of nanocomposites with either low (∼5–7at%) [5–9] or extreme high (∼16–20at%) [10,11] in the Pr-lean PrFeB series ribbons are normally employed to develop α-Fe/Pr2Fe14B and Fe3B/Pr2Fe14B types nanocomposites, respectively, by melt spinning [5–11], resulting in a lower intrinsic coercivity i H c of less than 8kOe, which limits their application at higher temperatures (T >100°C).

In our previous studies [12,13], higher i H c values of 8.3–14.3kOe, B r and (BH)max values of 9.4–9.6kG, and 15.7–16.2MGOe, respectively, have been obtained on the B-enriched ternary Pr9.5−11.76Febal.B10 ribbons, but a great deal of metastable R2Fe23B3 phase appears when the rare earth content is lower than 9at%. This reduces the volume fraction of R2Fe14B and deteriorates the magnetic properties drastically. Nevertheless, substitution of 2at% Ti for Fe in Pr-lean Pr2(Fe0.975Ti0.025)23B3 (R7.14(Fe0.975Ti0.025)82.14B10.72) leads to a remarkable improvement in the magnetic properties from B r =6.7kG, i H c =1.5kOe and (BH)max =1.7MGOe of the ternary Pr2Fe23B3 to B r =9.9kG, i H c =5.7kOe and (BH)max =15.0MGOe of the Pr2(Fe0.975Ti0.025)23B3 ribbons, owing to suppression of the metastable Pr2Fe23B3 phase and the formation of large amount of Pr2Fe14B and α-Fe phases with fine grain sizes [14]. In this paper, on a series of Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 (y =9.5, 9, 8.5, 8, 7.5, and 7) nanocomposite ribbons having a common factor of 10at% B, the effect of Pr content on the magnetic properties, microstructure and phase evolution of the ribbons will be studied. Comparison also will be made for the Ti-free and the Ti containing nanocomposite ribbons.

Alloy ingots with nominal compositions of Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 (y =9.5, 9, 8.5, 8, 7.5, and 7) were prepared by vacuum induction melting. Melt-spun ribbons were produced from the ingots with wheel speeds ranging from 10 to 30m/s. Selected ribbons were heat-treated at 600–700°C for 10min to optimize crystallization and in turn to improve the permanent magnetic properties. The various phases were identified by thermal magnetic analysis (TMA) (heating rate: 20°C/min). Furthermore, apart from a direct observation of their microstructures by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), phase identification was performed by energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX). The magnetic properties of the ribbons at room temperature were measured by a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM).

Fig. 1 illustrates magnetic properties as measured at 20°C of melt-spun Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 ribbons following their optimal crystallization treatment. Apparently, with decreasing Pr content, B r increases monotonically from 8.8kG for Pr9.5Fe78.5Ti2B10 to 9.9kG for Pr7Fe81Ti2B10, on the contrary, i H c decreases from 12.8kOe for Pr9.5Fe78.5Ti2B10 to 5.2kOe for Pr7Fe81Ti2B10. But (BH)max increases from 16.3 MGOe for y =9.5 to reach the maximum vale of 17.8MGOe for y =8.5, and then decreases to 14.5MGOe for y =7. It is noteworthy that the (BH)max of most of the above selected alloy ribbons is well above 16MGOe, even the ribbons with only 7at% Pr still attain the value of 14.5MGOe. It reflects that Ti atom is very effective in enhancing the magnetic properties of the alloy ribbons nearby Pr2Fe23B3 (Pr7.14Fe82.14B10.72)). The optimum magnetic properties of B r =9.6kG, i H c =8.5kOe, and (BH)max =17.8MGOe have been achieved in Pr8.5Fe79.5Ti2B10. Therefore, it would be of interest to elucidate the mechanism, by which the Pr content can affect both i H c and B r or (BH)max of the ribbons, through phase identification and microstructural studies.

The 2:23:3 and Fe3B phases in the higher boron-content matrix may form to complicate the phase analysis by X-ray diffraction (XRD); therefore, thermal magnetic analysis (TMA) is utilized. Fig. 2 depicts TMA of Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 ribbons. As can be seen, the magnetically hard 2:14:1 phase coexists with magnetically soft α-Fe phase in Pr9.5Fe78.5Ti2B10 nanocomposites. There are three phases, namely 2:14:1, α-Fe, and Fe3B, present in the ribbobs with y =9–7.5. For Pr7Fe81Ti2B10 nanocomposites, four phases, including 2:14:1, α-Fe, Fe3B, and 2:23:3, are found. With decreasing Pr content, the volume fraction of magnetically hard 2:14:1 phase reduces, on the contrary, that of magnetically soft phases increases. This explains why the remanence increases and the coercivity decreases with decreasing of Pr concentration.

Fig. 3 plots TEM images of Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 ribbons. Although multiple phases, including 2:14:1, α-Fe, Fe3B, or 2:23:3, might present in lower Pr content ribbons as detected by TMA shown in Fig. 2, it was very difficult to distinguish them unambiguously by TEM analysis. Nevertheless, it is apparent that the average grain size of ribbons coarsens slightly with decreasing Pr content. It is observed that the average grain size is estimated to be 10–20nm for y =9.5–8.5, 10–30nm for y =7.5, and 20–35nm for y =7, respectively.

According to Fischer et al. [15], an effective exchange-coupling interaction could exist between magnetic grains if the average grain size is smaller than 80nm. The intergranular exchange-coupling effects may enhance the remanence of the ribbons to a value larger than 4πM s/2, yielding a higher remanence for smaller grain size. The reduced remanence value (=M r/M 12kOe) of the ribbons studied was obtained by VSM, and it is 0.86 for y =9.5, 0.85 for y =9, 0.86 for y =8.5, 0.85 for y = 8, 0.84 for y =7.5, and 0.85 for y =7, respectively. The average grain size of all ribbons is quite fine (<35nm), which explains why the reduced remanence values of all ribbons in this study are far greater than 0.5.

From TEM studies, it is also found that grain boundary phase appears and its volume fraction gradually increases with the reduction of Pr content. For identifying the composition of the grain boundary phase, energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) is employed. The EDX result exhibits that the Ti atoms tend to appear at the grain boundary in this series ribbons. It is presumed that Ti prefers to react with excess boron to form Ti-boride in the grain boundary. On the other hand, since the amount of excess B increases with decreasing Pr content, it may result in the formation of larger volume fraction of grain boundary phase in the ribbons with lower Pr content.

For further understanding the exchange coupling effect between adjacent grains in the Pr8.5Fe79.5M2B10 ribbons, the Henkel plots (δM versus H) which have been successfully used by many researchers to study the nature of interactions between phases in nanocomposite magnets are employed [8,16–18]. The δM is defined as m d(H)−[1−2m r(H)], where m d is the reduced demagnetization remanence and m r the reduced magnetization remanence [19,20]. For magnetostatic interactions, δM is negative, on the contrary, the positive δM-peak height indicates the existence of exchange-coupling interaction either between grains, and the value of maximum δM reflects the strength of intergranular exchange coupling effect. Fig. 4 illustrates the δM plots as a function of the applied field for Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 ribbons. A positive δM is measured in all studied ribbons, confirming the existence of exchange coupling effect between grains. Obviously, the maximum of δM in the ribbons with y =9 and y =8.5 is larger than that with y =8, 7.5, and 7. Due to the coarser grain size and larger volume fraction of grain boundary phase, the Pr7Fe81Ti2B10, Pr7.5Fe80.5Ti2B10, and Pr8Fe80Ti2B10 ribbons exhibit weaker exchange-coupling interaction. However, with the reduction of Pr content, the effect of increasing volume fraction of magnetically soft phases is probably stronger than that of the reduction of intergranular exchange coupling, leading to an increment of the B r value.

In order to further realize the effect of Ti substitution, a comparison of magnetic properties of Pr x Fe90−x B10 (x =9.5–7) and Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 is shown in Fig. 5 . It is clear that the improved magnetic properties with higher intrinsic coercivity can be obtained in Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 nanocomposites compared with the Ti-free Pr x Fe90−x B10 (x =9.5–7) ribbons. The maximum magnetic properties of Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 nanocomposites can be obtained in the ribbons with lower rare earth content, i.e. x =9.5 for the Pr x Fe90−x B10 ribbons and y =8.5 for the Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 nanocomposites.

From the above results, this present paper shows the following: First, magnetic properties of the nanocomposites with y =9.5–8 can be higher than those of the commercial MQP-B+ powders (i H c =8–10kOe and (BH)max =14–15.7MGOe). Second, compared with similar compositions but Ti-free PrFeB series nanocomposites [12,13], Pr7−9.5Febal.Ti2B10 nanocomposites exhibit better magnetic properties and a larger applicable composition range, proving the beneficial effect of Ti [14,21]. Third, the magnetic properties are better and the applicable composition range is lager in the Pr7–9.5Febal.Ti2B10 nanocomposites than those of the corresponding NdFeTiB nanocomposites [22–25]. This could be due to a higher anisotropy field of Pr2Fe14B than that of Nd2Fe14B. Accordingly, Pr-less but boron-rich PrFeTiB alloy ribbons cannot only attain better magnetic properties than commercial powders but also extend the applicable composition range for making bonded magnets.

Even though Pr2Fe14B phase exhibits a higher anisotropy field, the Nd2Fe14B phase shows a higher saturated magnetization [26]. For further improving the magnetic properties, we therefore adopt Nd to substitute for Pr in the (Pr1−x Nd x )8.5Fe79.5Ti2B10 ribbons. Table 1 lists the magnetic properties of (Pr1−x Nd x )8.5Fe79.5Ti2B10, where x =0–0.5. It is shown that with increasing Nd content, B r increases from 9.6kG for x =0–9.9kG for x =0.5. On the contrary, i H c decreases from 8.5kOe for x =0–7.5kOe for x =0.5. The optimal magnetic properties of B r =9.9kG, i H c =8.0kOe, and (BH)max =18.7MGOe can be obtained in (Pr0.75Nd0.25)8.5Fe79.5Ti2B10 alloy ribbons.

Magnetic properties, phase evolution, and microstructure of melt spun Pr y Fe88−y Ti2B10 (y =9.5–7) nanocomposite ribbons have been reported. The decrease of i H c with decreasing Pr concentration arises from reduction in the volume fraction of the Pr2Fe14B phase. The increase of B r with decreasing Pr concentration is the consequence of the decrease in the volume fraction of the magnetically soft phases. An exchange coupling effect, as evidenced by Henkel plots, is found to exist in all nanocomposites and attributed to the finer grain size (<35nm). In this study, the optimal magnetic properties of B r =9.9kG, i H c =8.0kOe and (BH)max =18.7MGOe are achieved in (Pr0.75Nd0.25)8.5Fe79.5Ti2B10. The highest coercivity of i H c =12.8kOe (B r =8.8kG and (BH)max =16.3MGOe) is obtained in Pr9.5Fe78.5Ti2B10.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research financially supported by National Science Council, Taiwan under grant no. NSC-93-2112-M-194-001 is deeply appreciated.
